title: The Most Important Thing
subtitle: Uncommon Sense for the Thoughtful Investor
author: Howard Marks
themes: Second-level thinking is required to outperform the consensus
Understanding, recognizing, and controlling risk is the investor's primary job
Markets swing like a pendulum between euphoria and despair
Price is what you pay; value is what you get
Cycles are inevitable and predictable in direction but not timing
Knowing what you don't know is a competitive advantage
Defensive investing prioritizes survival over maximum returns
Luck plays a larger role than most investors acknowledge
awards: Warren Buffett blurb: 'This is that rarity, a useful book'
excerpt: There is no single most important thing in investing — there are twenty. Howard Marks, co-founder of Oaktree Capital Management, distills a lifetime of market experience into a philosophy centered on second-level thinking, risk control, cycle awareness, and the emotional discipline to act against the crowd. Not a how-to manual but a how-to-think framework.
